The next person we're planning, we're planning this event, I want to talk about the big shifts you can make in a business to go from six figures to seven figures and beyond. And I talked about the lifeline of a lead, I talked about some of the value ladder personality in your marketing, some of the things that I really spoke a lot of my time on. Mason spoke to you guys obviously about ways to get consistent traffic, how to buy traffic. Obviously there are a lot of ways to drive traffic to a website, but learning the consistent ones, the media buying, where you're spending X amount of money and you're making X amount on the other end, that's where you can really start growing and scaling your business. Some of it's kind of technical, like everything Mason says is way over my head, but it's good stuff to know, especially when you're trying to take your business from wherever you're at to the next level, because scaling your business, that kind of traffic he's talking about is how you really take it to the next level. Now the next person I wanted to introduce you guys to is, this is Garrett Robbins over here, and Garrett when I first met Garrett, when I first kind of started our business, I had it running for a while on my own, and then BJ, some of you guys may know BJ, he asked me for a job and I was like, oh sure, I never had any employees, but that'd be kind of fun to hang out with some of my friends during the day. So I invited BJ, he moved from Nebraska and started working with me, and he's like, hey, I got this guy named Garrett, he read Google Cash and he thinks it's really cool, he wants to talk to you on the phone. So I'm like, alright, so I call him up on the phone and I start talking to him, he's like, hey, can I move to Boise, I want to work with you. I'm like, I guess, how much money do you need to make? And he's like, well, he's like right now, I'm making over 100 grand during the summer selling security systems door to door, and I take the rest of your off kind of goof off. And I was like, 100 grand a year, I'm like, I can't pay you that much. He's like, that's cool, what can you pay me? I'm like, I can pay you about eight bucks an hour. He's like, alright, I'm in. And he moved to Boise to drop his $100,000 in three month job to work for eight bucks an hour. But it's been a lot of fun to have around. And first when we were getting into our business, we were trying out all sorts of stuff and trying things out. And somewhere along the way, I remember he told me, he's like, hey, we should try a call center. This is the internet, people don't buy stuff on the phones. There's no way that's ever going to fly. He's like, no, I swear. He's like, I used to work at a call center and people make tons of money on the phones. I can't believe you can make money on email. He's like, it's just really confusing. So we kind of these two different worlds. And we tried out a call center once and didn't really work the way, because I was kind of structuring the way I thought it was right. He came back probably a year later and said, look, there's a certain way we need to do this, the way we need to build our back and build our coaching program and stuff. We set this thing up. And this actually happened in a time in our business where we were kind of struggling. I had hired way too many of my close friends because I wanted more people to hang out with. And if anyone's ever hired a bunch of their friends to work for them, it's kind of scary. So we started getting in a lot of debt really, really quick. I was living off of credit cards. And we were actually pretty close to $200,000 in debt at the time. And I was like, crap, I got to fire all my friends or figure something out. Because I'm like, we're making money, but not as much money to cover payroll and all the other stuff that comes with when you start running a business. And so this is probably three or four Christmases ago. Garrett and I sat in the room together and we mapped out a coaching program and a call center. And we launched this thing. And after we launched it, him, he grabbed three or four other guys from the office and started making this makeshift call center. And we launched it. And the first week, first five or six days, they had sold 10 people a $5,500 coaching program. So in 10 days, we made $55,000, which covered Christmas payroll and a bunch of stuff. And then from there, our business kind of, we were able to grow really, really rapidly after we had some of those things in place. So what I wanted him to talk to you about today is some backend stuff and how to build your back end. Because again, that's where most of your profits will come in your business is the back end. You got to sell a lot of $30 ebooks to make any money. But you sell one or two, $5,000, $10,000 things, you make a lot of money really, really quick. So that's what he's going to be talking about, how to build that, how to do it, and how we do it here at Dotcom Secrets. So everyone, let's put our hands together for Garrett Robbins. Thank you. This is one of my parables for those who are wondering my parables, what you talked about earlier. So basically, when I was wrestling at BYU, my wrestling coach there was Mark Schultz and if you guys have ever seen a coach Schultz, he's about this tall, his arms are like down here and he's just like the toughest, meanest guy on earth and he's an Olympic champion wrestler. He had fought in the UFC and kicked the crap out of some like six foot six black dude. Just picked the guy up and just demolished him. Like the guy that you don't want to like mess with, right? So my first day at BYU, we're sitting there and I went to the restroom and you know, I spent some time with him and I went back home to my dorm and then four or five hours later, I was sitting there and I also heard knock on the door and he opened the door, I opened the door and it was Coach Schultz and I'm like, hey, what's going on? And he's like, hey, I got a DVD for you. He hands me a DVD. I'm like, thanks. He's like, go get your wallet. I'm like, all right. So I go get my wallet and bring it back to him and he opens it up and he takes all my money. It's like 12 bucks. I took all my money out of my pocket, shoves it around my wallet, shoves his pocket, hands my wallet back and I was like, Coach, what are you doing? And he's like, I'm going to teach you a lesson right now that will serve you for the rest of your life. He said, if I were just giving you that DVD, you would have put it up on yourself and you never would have done anything with it. He said, because now you've invested in that DVD, you're going to watch it over and over and over again and you'll become a better wrestler and a better person because of it. And it was true. I've watched it over and over because I had now something, I had some skin in the game that made me actually want to go and take action on it. Very, very important. You said that was one of your parables? Is that what you're saying? Very important parable. If I'm going to go to your site right now and I'm going to buy something from you, the cheapest thing I can buy from you, who are interested in that information or that product, what would happen to me next? And if I bought that, then what would happen to me? If I bought that, then where would I be driven to? And so on and so on. Draw that system out. Make sure you're very clear on that. I can't tell you how many people I've coached that said, this stuff doesn't work and I'm so upset and I ask one question. If I want to go to your site right now and enter my credit card number and you will get paid for that, how would I do it? Why don't have anything set up that way? That's the whole purpose of the thing that we're going to go through today. It's the same thing for people who have a business, I can't do a back end program. If I wanted to buy your high ticket item right now, how would I do it? If I don't have one? Well, that's why no one's buying it. Okay? So it's all about creating. But say if this is like the funnel, and my back end system is the same, like let me, let's just take a niche, let's take the sports niche. This is beautiful for you actually. So if this is the end result, and you're mostly baseball driven, but let's just say it's overall sports driven. Okay? Then gosh, I mean, I got football, right? I got baseball. I got hockey. I mean, whatever. So all these are different pipelines driving people down to maybe my generic offer, right? So sports success for football, sports success for baseball. So what I was doing was control F, find a word, replace with word, and it's the same book, same stuff, but for different thing. And so, but it's all coming down to the exact same thing. So I'm driving traffic from all these different avenues, all these different pipelines coming down to my funnel to get them up to one, the whole goal guys is to push them down further and further. And then I'm going to do the extension model idea, which actually is better because you know, you're pushing them up rather than down. All right? Then it's the same, it's the same concept. Okay? And then I take the audios and do it for my free, you know, you can take, you know, I'm sure Russ talked about this already, or you've heard him talk about it, regurgitating content. It's just the same content, just delivering it in a different fashion, different manner. Okay? Next slide, we're going to skip. Slide 11. Okay. 12, I'm going to talk about this a little bit. It was really cool to have Mason talk right before, because it's really all about this. It's about leads, 100%. Everything that you, you know, the only purpose of your guys' websites, and the only purpose of your marketing should be for one reason, and that's to create a lead. Okay? You create a lead. Now, you might be thinking, well, what if I don't want to lead? What if I want to buy her? Great. As long as you have a back end, your buyer's a lead. Okay? That's the only reason. Everything should be, you know, qualified prospects, getting them qualified, driving leads. And as long as you have your system, and let's just say, I mean, let's look at the math. Let's say it costs me, you know, let's say it costs me $2 to produce a front end lead. Okay? $2 to produce a front end lead. And you guys can do the math, but let's just say, so it's $2, and I do this, and I create 200 leads. Okay? So how much have I spent right there? 400 bucks. Okay? So I spent 400 bucks, but let's say in the back end, each lead is worth $50 because I've created a back end system. Okay? Now, it doesn't mean that every lead is going to give me $50. That's not what I'm saying. I'm saying maybe two of those leads give me $10,000 or whatever that math ends up being. Okay? So I'm saying that each lead, the DPL, the dollar per lead is $50. So all more than half a lease go negative $2 to pop out at the end, positive $48. All right? You do that all day. I mean, who wouldn't? You know, you put one in, you get four back, of course. But that's why it's important to have a back end system. Who already has one, by the way? Who already has a back end marketing system with their business? You do? What's your price point? $500 an hour. $500 an hour? And is it you doing it? Okay. Gotcha. Did you, so another hand go up? Yeah, I did the package for $950. Okay, so for, yeah, little coaching. Is it one-on-one? Yeah. And you do it? Uh-huh. Is it group as well? No, I don't have a group. You don't do group coaching as well? You should do that one-on-one? No, I will. But I don't have it yet. Gotcha. Okay. All right. And it's $1,000 to do it. So you know what your metrics are? You know what your metrics are? You know what your metrics are? I mean, how much does it cost you? I know. Okay. All right. Okay. So that's the whole purpose of the leads. So slide number 13, how should I qualify my leads? Okay. This is important to understand. It's kind of like the funnel thing or the essential model. Let them bump. It's very important. It's the whole concept of I only have time to spend with 10 people a day. I want to make sure I'm spending time with the best 10 people a day. Okay. Who's done, obviously you're doing network marketing. Who's done network marketing here? Okay. Who's been on the phone? You call them through leads and try to qualify people and get them going. Okay. How awesome is that? Isn't that sweet? Awesome. But how much awesomer would it be if you had 10 people that were trying to call you, but you have to leave a message saying, you know what, I only work these hours. I'll call you back when I can. And they, and your assistant schedules an appointment for you, and they're saying at the phone waiting for you to call. Would that be better? That would be better. Yes. Okay. So obviously use your marketing system, use your site to allow that to happen. Allow it to happen. I mean, when we first started this thing, I know that we were looking at a software or a script that would allow people to get online. And we never used it, and we still should, but instead of having an assistant do it, but they could get on and they could say, all right, here's the time slots available, and I want my consultation or my sales pitch to be done at this time. And so they'd pick the time to be home, and then it would send an email out to their outlook or a calendar and it would shoot an email an hour before, a day before, and like a week before, and whatever of the time that basically is like a flag that remind them, you're going to get sold at this time. And so, you know, there are systems out there that's really, really useful. Okay, letting them age. Okay. Now let's, who ships out a physical information product? Physical information product. Okay. Let's take you, for example, again, when you ship that out, how long does it take to get there? That's right where they are in the country, but three to four days. Three to four days, okay. Now if you were to call them on the fifth day, your audience might be different than the audience that you generally work with, but would that be good or bad? To start selling them the next offer. Yeah. It wouldn't be good, right? You need to let it age a little bit, right? Leads are kind of like wine. You got to let them age a little bit, get better and better. Because the idea guys is, you know, they buy the information and they have it sitting on the kitchen table and then the wife gets really upset because it's been there for three days and nothing's happened with it and she's getting dinner ready. And then she puts it over here on the bookshelf over here and then a week goes by and he sees it there or whoever sees it there and they're like, I got to get to that. I got to get to that. And then you get a phone call offering help. Okay. And that's the, those are the best ways. And sometimes we age things up to 40 days and sometimes we age things like a week. It just kind of depends on what it is. If it's digital, then obviously we don't have to wait for shipping and it's not a physical product. We're aging things different, but when doing a back-end program, you got to take the aging of the leading consideration because it is very important. I mean, we'll triple the volume that we'll do if we age something properly than not. I mean, it's a science and it's, I would never think it would be as important that it is, but it is.
